description: Hand-authored negative-control corpus for T5 of the bench-hardening worktrail (docs/BENCH_HARDENING_FROM_QCVV.md). Twenty documents engineered to violate the canonicalisation pipeline's assumptions across five failure modes (four per mode). Each document is annotated with its expected failure mode + expected behaviour. A negative-control test passes when observed failures match annotations — i.e., the bench correctly fails on inputs it should fail on, AND succeeds on inputs it should succeed on. A bench with no documented failure mode is not a benchmark.

documents.text: Alice told Beth she had won.
documents.expected_behavior: Either 'won(Alice)' OR 'won(Beth)' is extractable; the pronoun 'she' is syntactically ambiguous. A faithful extractor either drops the claim, surfaces both candidates, or commits to one with low confidence. Silent commitment to one without flagging is the failure mode this case probes.

documents.text: The lawyer told the witness he was lying.
documents.expected_behavior: 'he' could refer to the lawyer or the witness. Same shape as amb_coref_01. Silent commitment is failure.

documents.text: When Marie met Sophie, she had just published a paper.
documents.expected_behavior: 'she' is anaphoric but referent is unresolved. Sieve will likely drop; LLM might commit silently.

documents.text: The CEO told the journalist his term was ending.
documents.expected_behavior: 'his term' could be the CEO's (typical reading) or the journalist's. The typical reading is plausible but not certain.

documents.text: Alice authored the report. Alice wrote the report.
documents.expected_behavior: 'authored' and 'wrote' are aliases of the same relation but lemmatise / canonicalise to different predicates. The extractor may produce two distinct triples (authored, wrote) or one canonicalised triple. Either is acceptable; SILENT canonical-collapse without surfacing the original predicate phrasing is the failure to detect.

documents.text: The drug treats migraines. The drug cures migraines.
documents.expected_behavior: 'treats' and 'cures' are semantically near but NOT aliases — 'cures' is stronger. A canonicaliser that collapses them is wrong; a canonicaliser that preserves the distinction is right. Probes whether the predicate ontology distinguishes strength of claim.

documents.text: Bob owns the company. Bob founded the company. Bob runs the company.
documents.expected_behavior: Three different relations to the same (Bob, company) pair. Loss of any one is a fact-preservation failure.

documents.text: The model was trained on the data. The model learned from the data.
documents.expected_behavior: 'was trained on' and 'learned from' are colloquially equivalent in ML but technically distinct verb-frames. Probes the technical-vs-colloquial canonicalisation tension.

documents.text: Alice was born in 1990. Alice was born in 1991.
documents.expected_behavior: Two contradictory birth-year claims about the same entity. A faithful canonicaliser produces TWO triples (preserving the contradiction so a consistency check can flag it) — NOT silently picking one. The axiom_consistency_check field on the bundle should report inconsistent=true.

documents.text: The company is profitable. The company is operating at a loss.
documents.expected_behavior: 'profitable' and 'operating at a loss' are mutually exclusive. Same expectation as contradict_01.

documents.text: Senator Kerr voted for the bill. Senator Kerr voted against the bill.
documents.expected_behavior: Direct contradiction in voting record. Same expectation.

documents.text: The patient is allergic to penicillin. The patient tolerates penicillin well.
documents.expected_behavior: Medical contradiction with safety implication. The consistency check is critical here — silent collapse would propagate a clinical risk.

documents.text: Relativity is a foundational concept.
documents.expected_behavior: 'Relativity' could be Einstein's physics theory, the music label, or a general concept. The /api/qid resolver picks one; the bench should surface that the choice is ambiguous. (The README's PROOF_BOUNDARY notes the 'music label vs physics concept' case at the qid resolution floor.)

documents.text: Apple released a new product on Tuesday.
documents.expected_behavior: 'Apple' could be the company or the fruit (in context, clearly the company; out of context, ambiguous). Probes whether disambiguation uses the rest of the sentence.

documents.text: Mercury rose to 100 degrees.
documents.expected_behavior: 'Mercury' could be the planet, the element, the Roman god, or a thermometer reading. Context ('rose to 100 degrees') suggests temperature. Probes whether numeric context disambiguates.

documents.text: Java is widely used in industry.
documents.expected_behavior: 'Java' could be the island, the coffee, or the programming language. Without further context the programming-language reading is most plausible in technical corpora; in a travel corpus, the island wins. Probes whether corpus-level priors matter.

documents.text: Should we trust AI-generated content?
documents.expected_behavior: Question, not assertion. The extractor should produce zero triples. A faulty extractor might produce 'we trust AI_generated_content' or similar — that's the failure mode.

documents.text: Suppose Alice had won the election.
documents.expected_behavior: Counterfactual — explicitly NOT asserting that Alice won. Extracting 'won(Alice)' would be a fact-preservation failure.

documents.text: Some researchers believe the model is overfitting.
documents.expected_behavior: Hedged claim ('some researchers believe'). The claim is about belief, not about overfitting. A faulty extractor might produce 'model overfit'; the right extraction is either zero triples or 'researchers believe overfitting'.

documents.text: If the bill passes, the markets will react.
documents.expected_behavior: Conditional, not assertion. Neither clause is asserted as true. Extracting 'bill_passes' or 'markets_react' as factual would be the failure.
